paper_title: The Dual Reader Standard for Software: Measurement-Theoretic Falsification Applied to Executable Systems

phase_ready.note: MK-P6 AI layer. 2 placeholders: PH-MK6.1 (independent empirical validation pending), PH-MK6.2 (formal proof of Property 7.1 pending). NOT-PHASE-READY: empirical validation is blocking.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Popperian epistemological foundation
claim_registry.statement: Falsificationism (Popper, 1959) as the epistemological basis for software verification: we can falsify behavioral claims but not verify them with certainty. This is deliberately weaker than formal verification and deliberately more tractable.
claim_registry.axiomatic_basis: Popper, K. R. (1959). The Logic of Scientific Discovery.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: DRS Layer 0 kernel applicability
claim_registry.statement: The falsification kernel K = (P, O, M, B) as defined in falsification-kernel.md v1.1 is applicable to software behavioral claims. The grammar (Section 3), operand specification (Section 4), mechanism requirements (Section 5), and bounds specification (Section 6) of the kernel all apply to executable systems.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Design by Contract foundation
claim_registry.statement: Design by Contract (Meyer, 1992) established preconditions, postconditions, and invariants as executable specifications. The DRS for Software extends, not replaces, DbC.
claim_registry.axiomatic_basis: Meyer, B. (1992). Applying Design by Contract. IEEE Computer, 25(10).

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Software Claim Taxonomy
claim_registry.statement: Software claims are classified into three types: Type A (Assumptions — platform requirements, dependency contracts, environmental preconditions), Type D (Definitions — type signatures, data structures, configuration schemas), Type F (Behavioral Claims — correctness guarantees, invariants, performance bounds, API contracts).

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Falsification Completeness
claim_registry.statement: A software system S with claim registry R is falsification-complete iff: (1) every publicly documented behavior corresponds to at least one Type F claim, (2) every Type F claim has a well-formed K predicate, (3) every Type F claim evaluates to NOT FALSIFIED, (4) every unverified claim is registered as a placeholder.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Software Phase-Ready Verdict
claim_registry.statement: A software release is PHASE-READY iff c1 (public API coverage), c2 (well-formed predicates), c3 (all evaluations pass), c4 (dependencies resolved or placeholdered), c5 (placeholder documentation), c6 (schema validity) are all SATISFIED.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Three gap categories
claim_registry.statement: Applying DRS to existing software reveals three categories of gap: Category 1 (tested but unbounded — BOUNDARY gaps), Category 2 (documented but untested — placeholder gaps), Category 3 (assumed but unregistered — Type A gaps).

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Kernel universality — K applies to software without modification
claim_registry.statement: The falsification kernel K = (P, O, M, B) as defined in falsification-kernel.md v1.1 applies to software behavioral claims without modification. The same grammar, evaluation semantics, and validity constraints that govern scientific Type F claims govern software Type F claims.

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS software_claim C SUCH THAT is_falsifiable(C) = TRUE AND is_expressible_as_K(C) = FALSE

WHERE.C: string · a behavioral claim about software that is empirically falsifiable by testing
WHERE.is_falsifiable: binary · TRUE if C can be contradicted by an observable test outcome
WHERE.is_expressible_as_K: binary · TRUE if C can be expressed as a well-formed (FALSIFIED_IF, WHERE, EVALUATION, BOUNDARY, CONTEXT) 4-tuple satisfying all constraints in falsification-kernel.md Sections 3–6

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Attempt to express each of the 7 Type F claims in SFW-1 v2 as K 4-tuples; verify all are well-formed; if any cannot be expressed, identify the kernel constraint that prevents expression; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: any software claim that is falsifiable but inexpressible as K → FALSIFIED; all 7 expressible → NOT FALSIFIED for the demonstration scope
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: The kernel constraints (determinism, finiteness, no self-reference, completeness of operands) are general enough to accommodate any finite test procedure. The claim is that no software-specific extension to the kernel grammar is needed.

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: A software claim 'this function is elegant' is falsifiable (subjective judgment could disagree) but cannot be expressed as K because elegance has no deterministic predicate → BUT this claim fails the is_falsifiable test (subjective claims are not empirically falsifiable) → does not FALSIFY. A genuine counterexample would be: 'this function handles 10,000 concurrent requests' is falsifiable but cannot be expressed in K grammar → would FALSIFY if K grammar cannot express concurrency load tests.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Falsification completeness implies coverage
claim_registry.statement: If a software system S is falsification-complete with respect to claim registry R, then there exists a test suite T derived from evaluation procedures in R such that L(S, T) >= L(S, T') for any T' achieving the same behavioral verification.

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS system S AND registry R SUCH THAT falsification_complete(S, R) = TRUE AND coverage(T_R) < coverage(T_alt) WHERE behavioral_verification(T_alt) = behavioral_verification(T_R)

WHERE.R: set · claim registry for S satisfying Definition 7.2
WHERE.T_R: set · test suite derived from EVALUATION procedures in R
WHERE.T_alt: set · any alternative test suite with same behavioral verification
WHERE.behavioral_verification: function · set of behavioral claims verified by T

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Construct a falsification-complete system with registry; derive test suite from evaluations; measure coverage; compare against alternative suites; finite for bounded systems
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: coverage(T_R) < coverage(T_alt) with same verification → FALSIFIED
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: This formalizes the intuition that testing every claimed behavior covers more code than testing arbitrary paths. The property is about the relationship between claim-driven and path-driven testing.

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: A system where claim-derived tests cover 80% of lines but a random test suite with same behavioral verification covers 95% → FALSIFIED

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Coverage does not imply falsification completeness
claim_registry.statement: There exist software systems with L(S, T) = 1.0 (100% line coverage) that are not falsification-complete.

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: FOR ALL systems S WITH L(S, T) = 1.0, falsification_complete(S, R_derived) = TRUE

WHERE.T: set · test suite achieving 100% line coverage
WHERE.R_derived: set · claim registry derivable from T

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Construct the counterexample from Property 7.2: f(x)=x+1 with test assert f(2)==3. Verify 100% coverage. Verify behavioral claim 'f(x)=x+1 for all x' is not falsification-complete because test checks only one input; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: if every 100%-coverage system is automatically falsification-complete → FALSIFIED; one counterexample suffices → NOT FALSIFIED
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: The counterexample is constructive. The distinction between 'code runs' and 'code does what it claims' is the core insight.

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: If no system with 100% coverage can fail to be falsification-complete, the property is falsified. But f(x)=x+1 with test f(2)==3 is a concrete counterexample.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Gap detection — DRS reveals gaps invisible to coverage
claim_registry.statement: Applying the DRS to Fracttalix Sentinel v12.1 reveals at least one claim in each of the three gap categories (tested-but-unbounded, documented-but-untested, assumed-but-unregistered) that is invisible to conventional line/branch coverage metrics.

WHERE.category_1_gaps: integer · count of claims with passing tests but incomplete BOUNDARY documentation in SFW-1 v2
WHERE.category_2_gaps: integer · count of README/docstring promises with no corresponding Type F claim in SFW-1 v2
WHERE.category_3_gaps: integer · count of dependency behavioral assumptions not registered as Type A in SFW-1 v1 (pre-DRS)

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Audit SFW-1 v2 claim registry; count placeholder claims (Category 2); count new Type A claims not in v1 (Category 3); count Type F claims with BOUNDARY notes indicating untested edges (Category 1); finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: zero gaps in any category → FALSIFIED; at least one in each category → NOT FALSIFIED
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: SFW-1 v1 had 10 claims. SFW-1 v2 has 20 claims including 5 Type A (all new — Category 3 confirmed), 4 placeholders (Category 2 confirmed). Category 1 requires boundary audit of existing tests.

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: SFW-1 v2 has zero new Type A claims → category_3_gaps = 0 → FALSIFIED. Actual: 5 new Type A claims → NOT FALSIFIED.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Feasibility — DRS applicable in one session
claim_registry.statement: The DRS for Software can be applied to a 37-step, 374-test streaming pipeline to produce a valid software AI layer within a single working session, without specialized formal methods tooling.

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: session_count > 1 OR specialized_tools_required = TRUE OR schema_validation_fails = TRUE

WHERE.session_count: integer · number of sessions required to produce SFW-1 v2
WHERE.specialized_tools_required: binary · TRUE if any tool beyond standard editor, git, Python, and JSON validator was needed
WHERE.schema_validation_fails: binary · TRUE if SFW-1 v2 fails validation against ai-layer-schema.json

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Verify SFW-1 v2 was produced in Session 51; verify only standard tools used; validate against schema; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: multiple sessions → FALSIFIED; any specialized formal methods tool required → FALSIFIED; schema failure → FALSIFIED
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Tractability is the key DRS positioning claim. If applying DRS requires the same expertise as formal verification, the tractability gap argument fails.

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: SFW-1 v2 required Coq proof assistant to formalize claims → specialized_tools_required = TRUE → FALSIFIED

placeholder_register.description: Property 7.1 (falsification completeness implies coverage) is stated as a formal property but proven only by argument sketch, not formal proof. Independent empirical validation on multiple codebases required.
placeholder_register.target_session: OPEN — requires empirical validation per Section 11

placeholder_register.description: Gap detection claim verified only on authors' own software (Sentinel). Independent application to 3+ external projects required per empirical agenda.
